title: Emergency Guide: My Dog Ate Raisins - Immediate Steps
meta_desc: Raisins are highly toxic to dogs and can cause sudden kidney failure. If your dog ate raisins, this is a high-urgency emergency. Call a vet now.
content_html:
TL;DR: Raisin ingestion is a high-priority emergency for dogs as it can cause rapid kidney failure even in small amounts. Seek immediate veterinary attention if any ingestion occurs.

What is raisin toxicity in dogs and why is it so dangerous?

Grapes and their dried counterparts, raisins, are highly toxic to dogs. Even a small amount can trigger an idiosyncratic reaction that leads to acute kidney failure. Unlike other toxins where the dose is strictly based on weight, some dogs can be severely affected by just one or two raisins, making any ingestion a serious concern.

Should I take my dog to the vet immediately if they eat a raisin?

  • Yes, the urgency level for raisin ingestion is High.
  • Because there is no established safe dose and kidney damage can be rapid, do not wait for symptoms to appear.
  • Immediate veterinary intervention, such as induced vomiting or the administration of activated charcoal, is most effective when performed shortly after ingestion.

How can taking a photo of the raisins help my dog's triage?

  • If possible, take a photo of the raisins or the packaging they came in.
  • Providing a visual helps your veterinarian estimate the volume consumed.
  • A photo allows the vet to check for other dangerous additives, such as chocolate coating or xylitol.
  • This information allows the clinical team to prepare the correct treatment protocol before you even arrive at the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Following ingestion of grapes or raisins, prompt decontamination is recommended. For asymptomatic dogs with recent ingestion of more than 1-2 g/kg, emesis should be induced using 3% hydrogen peroxide (2 mL/kg, not exceeding 45 mL), followed by administration of activated charcoal, particularly with large ingestions. In cases involving large ingestions or spontaneous vomiting and/or diarrhea within 12 hours of ingestion, aggressive fluid diuresis for a minimum of 48 hours is recommended, with close monitoring of renal function and fluid balance. It is important to consider other causes of renal failure in the differential diagnosis.

Chapter: Toxicology, Nephrology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2968)

faqs.question: How many raisins are toxic to a dog?
faqs.answer: There is no known safe amount. Because some dogs are more sensitive than others, even a single raisin can cause kidney failure in certain individuals.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of raisin poisoning?
faqs.answer: Early symptoms include vomiting, diarrhea, and lethargy. As kidney failure progresses, you may notice increased thirst, followed by a total lack of urination.

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: Never induce vomiting at home without direct instruction from a veterinarian or pet poison control expert, as it can lead to aspiration pneumonia if performed incorrectly.
